content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 1, 1995

Honorable Janet Leahy
Councilmember
City of San Jacinto
990 Chase Street
San Jacinto, California 92582

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-038

Dear Ms. Leahy:

This is in reply to your request for advice concerning your
duties as Councilmember for the City of San Jacinto under the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.

QUESTIONS

1. Would you have a conflict of interest, or would you be
aiding and abetting in a conflict-of-interest violation, if you
participate in a decision where another official has a conflict of
interest under the Act?

2. Do you have a duty to report possible conflict-of-
interest violations under the Act?

3. Is the name of a complainant kept confidential?

CONCLUSIONS

1. You would not have a conflict of interest, and would not
be aiding and abetting another official to violate the Act, if you
participate in a decision in which you do not have a conflict of
interest. However, the governmental decision is voidable if any
councilmember who has a conflict of interest makes, participates
in the making, or influences that decision.

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000 - 18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-95-038
Page 2


2.  You do not have a duty under the Act to report possible
conflict-of-interest violations.

3.  Access to information concerning complaints filed with
the Commission are granted in accordance with the dictates of the
Public Records Act.  (Sections 6250, et seq.  Generally, the
names of complainants are kept confidential.

ANALYSIS

1.  Conflicts of Interest Based on the Conflict of Interest
of Another Participating Councilmember.

You have stated that you are a councilmember for the City of
San Jacinto and you do not have a financial interest in a decision
pending before the city council.  Nevertheless, you are concerned
that you may have a conflict of interest, or may be aiding and
abetting in the commission of a violation under the Act, if
another councilmember who does have a conflict of interest
participates in the decision.

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise attempting to use his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.²  Thus, this provision
prohibits a public official from participating in a governmental
decision if the official has a conflict of interest with respect
to that decision; it does not, however, prohibit another
councilmember from participating in the decision.  Accordingly,
you do not have a conflict of interest if a councilmember who has
a conflict of interest in a decision participates in that
decision.  (Kloecker Advice Letter, No. I-95-018.)  Moreover,
merely participating in the decision would not constitute aiding
and abetting under the Act.  (Section 83116.5.)³

2.  Duty to Report.

You also ask whether you have a duty to report apparent
violations of the Act.  The Act imposes a specific duty upon
filing officers to report apparent violations of the Act to the

_______

2  Regulations 18700 and 18700.1 define the terms "make,"
"participate in making," and "attempting to use his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision."  Under
those definitions, a public official who is disqualified from
participating in a decision, generally, is also prohibited from
participating in discussions concerning those decisions.

3  Section 83116.5 provides that any person who purposely or
negligently causes any other person to violate any provision of
this title, or who aids and abets any other person in the
violation of any provision of this title, shall be liable for
administrative penalties under the Act.

Our File No. A-95-038
Page 3


appropriate agencies.  (Section 81010 and Regulation 18115.)
However, this requirement pertains only to those officials who are
filing officers.  No similar requirement is imposed on other
officials.

     However, please note that, although the Act does not impose
upon you a duty to report apparent violations of the Act, it would be in the
public interest to report violations of the Act. Moreover,
Section 91003 provides in pertinent part that where a court
determines a violation of the Act's conflict-of-interest
provisions has occurred and the decision "might not otherwise have
been taken or approved," the court may set the decision aside as
void.  (Section 91003(b).)  Therefore, if a councilmember does in
fact have a conflict of interest in making, participating in, or
influencing a governmental decision, the decision is potentially
voidable as set forth under Section 91003.

     3.  Confidentiality of Complainant's Name.

     You also ask whether a complainant's name is confidential.
As a general rule, it is the policy of the Commission not to
disclose the names of complainants and any other information which
may identify a complainant.  Access to complaints filed with the
Commission, responses thereto, and investigative files and
information are granted in accordance with the dictates of the
Public Records Act.  (Sections 6250, et seq.; Regulation 18362.)

     Thus, for example, the name of a complainant may be disclosed
if the complainant has waived confidentiality.  This could also be
the case if a public official publicly announces that he or she
has filed a complaint with the Commission or discloses his or her
identity as part of a formal complaint with the Commission under
Regulation 18360(b)⁴ (copy attached).

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please call me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Luisa Menchaca
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure

_________________________
4     A person may file an informal complaint, but only the filing of
a formal complaint entitles the complainant with certain
procedural rights set forth in Section 83115 and Regulation 18360.
